message.content:
The door doesn’t open so much as give way.

It sticks halfway, something soft wedged against the floor. When you shove harder, it peels free with a thick, sucking sound, like a boot dragged out of deep mud. A smear of dark, half-dried red traces under the threshold as the gap widens.

The smell hits first.

It isn’t just “bad.” It’s a wet, rotting heaviness that seems to have a temperature, a humid, clinging heat that rushes into your nose and mouth at once. Sweet at the edges, like spoiled fruit, but undercut with something metallic and sour that makes your tongue prickle. Each breath feels like swallowing meat gone rancid weeks ago. Your eyes water without your permission. Your stomach twitches.

Flies own this room.

You hear them before you see anything: a dense, layered buzzing, high and low, a living static that fills your ears. It comes in waves as they lift and settle, a constant restless shiver of wings. Underneath that, there’s another sound, softer: the faint wet tick of something dripping, the occasional lazy plop as a drop falls from somewhere above to somewhere below.

The light is wrong. A naked bulb hangs from a wire, swinging almost imperceptibly in a draft you can’t feel. Its dim, yellow glow stutters, the filament whining in protest, throwing the room into a jittery sequence of still images whenever it flickers. The shadows move more than the bulb does.

At first your mind refuses to make sense of what you’re seeing.

Shapes pile against one another on the floor and along the walls, a jumble of pale and dark, fabric and flesh. It looks like a heap of discarded mannequins—until your eyes pick out a fingernail, still painted a chipped pink. A wristwatch, strapped to nothing. Hair, tangled and matted, glued to the floor by congealed blood.

There are bodies everywhere, but not as bodies anymore.

Limbs lie at wrong angles, not from injury but because they are no longer connected to anything. An arm drapes over a toppled chair, the hand hanging loose, fingers curved as if reaching for help that never came. A leg rests on top of a torso that isn’t its own, the exposed bone at its end dull and yellowed, the ragged meat around it darkened to a nearly black crust. You recognize the curve of a shoulder, the slope of a neck, but they belong to different people now, shuffled and stacked like pieces of something ruined and unsolvable.

The floor is a patchwork of stains and wetness. Some of the blood has dried into dark, rusty smears; some is newer, tacky and shiny, glistening in the bulb’s feeble light. Where you step, your shoe sinks slightly, the sole sticking to the floor with a soft, obscene adhesion. When you lift your foot, it comes free with a quiet, revolted “skrrp,” like pulling tape from skin. Something slick and stringy clings to the edge of your shoe for a moment before letting go and folding back onto the ground.

You take another hesitant step and something under your heel isn’t just wet; it has give. The texture is horribly specific—the thin resistance of skin over soft tissue, then a sudden collapse. There’s a muffled, jellylike crunch, and you know, with a sick, icy certainty, that you’ve stepped on something that was once inside someone.

Viscous puddles lap at the edges of the room, reflecting the jaundiced light. Mixed in with the blood are bits you can’t help but identify: a coil of something pale and ropey, glistening and looped like a dropped length of sausage; a lumpy, purple mass that might once have pulsed with life; a thin sheet of something off-white, shredded and torn, clinging to the leg of a table. They don’t look like organs the way they do in textbooks or movies. They look wrong out here, uncontained, slumping over one another like discarded, spoiled food.

The flies swarm thickest over these piles. They cluster in frantic black knots, crawling and crawling in place, their tiny legs combing over the surfaces, disappearing into folds and crevices and reemerging, heavier. When your shadow falls over them, some lift, rising in a sudden, furious cloud that explodes upward, the sound swelling into a shrill, angry roar that seems to vibrate in your teeth. A few land on your skin. Their feet are impossibly light and deliberate as they test your exposed wrist, your neck. You swat them away and your hand comes back smeared with something you don’t want to see.

Among the heaps, faces emerge at odd angles.

One
